speaker: interviewer
text: OK, stand by. And we have speed. This is the fifth tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so one last question. What are you doing now, in Atlanta? Or what did you-- what have you done since you came to Atlanta?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Throughout the first 15 years, I was working in different jobs, odd jobs, you know? I was-- some places I was successful. Some places I was not successful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- in the beginning, I was working for $1.63 an hour scrubbing rust and sweeping floors in a machine shop. I was working for Atlanta News Agency, packing books, receiving books, and distributing to the stores. I was working in a meat-packing company, in a freezer. I was working-- I was working for Piccadilly Cafeterias, starting from dish washing and ending with-- with manager trainee. I was working for-- for-- for-- for a collection agency, who I used to go to the to Southwest Atlanta and try to collect money from delinquent people. And sometimes they would show me a fist in front of my nose. But all the time, I tried and I dreamed and I was teaching, teaching, teaching. I gave lectures about the Holocaust in different organizations, in churches, in synagogues, and different civic organizations. I was talking about communistic society. I was-- I was teaching evenings, Yiddish, at the Jewish community center. At the Ahavath Achim Synagogue, I was teaching Yiddish. I was teaching at Beth Jacob, Yiddish. And I'm teaching sometimes. I was talking about-- and I was nostrificating my diploma, because the year before we left Poland, I received my master's degree in Russian Philology in Poland, finally. And I nostrificate my diploma in United States, in Georgetown University, after which I strived to go back to teach. Atlanta did not offer teaching Russian. Finally, in 1984, in the Cobb County School System, was-- was an opening of-- of a magnet program. They start to teach Chinese and Russian. They needed a Russian teacher. A friend of mine and the principal of Lakeside High School-- where my kids later went and they started there-- called me up and he said, listen, Jay, you have a job, probably. Hopefully, it's full-time. Go ahead and see this person. And that's how I was hired to be a Russian teacher and a teacher of world history. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I finished my studying at Georgia State University. I received my certificate, beside master's degree, a certificate. And I'm with the Cobb County School System until now. I was teaching for-- for the-- for a Jewish high school, evening school, also Yiddish, here, in Atlanta. I was teaching at Emory University, part-time-- Yiddish, Polish, and Russian. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am-- I was and I am an interpreter and translator of-- of Russian and Polish. I was translating for-- for CNN. And during the crisis in Poland, I was there. Called several times to translate what the Polish news agency is talking-- I was translating for [? Gromyko ?] when he gave-- he gave a news conference and I was in CNN, translating his words. I was-- I was interpreter for-- for the-- for-- for the Hawks game, for the Hawks who-- who invited a Russian team from-- a Russian basketball team. And I was traveling with them, translating. I was interpreter for the Goodwill Games in Seattle. I was teaching Russian for the crew of the Goodwill Games in Atlanta, teaching Russian for five years. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you got anything coming up with the Olympics?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. for-- In the Olympics, I'm waiting for an answer that I might be, during the Olympics, an interpreter of Polish and Russian for the Coca-Cola Company. They-- they-- they are waiting for a lot of people coming from over there, and I will be translating. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I guess my last question would then be, is there anything you have to say, anything you want to say about where you've come from and the way your life has turned out? And just anything you want to say about the way you feel about the way things have gone for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am satisfied with my life. I'm satisfied with being here United States, even though went through crisis. And everybody goes through crisis, no matter what country you're coming to, without-- with not knowledge of the language at all, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Future? I'm-- I'm waiting for-- for-- for-- for some good grandchildren, but my-- my-- that my wife will be healthy, and I will end my life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seeing my wife and seeing my children happy. My concerns are that we are, right now, going through a different Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A lot of intermarriages, which-- which is great. In Germany, before Hitler, 45% were also intermarriages. I think this is-- this is horrible. And it's-- it's not because I'm against-- against another, you know? This one-- and secondly, unfortunately, it's not dead, it's still alive and well-- but not the way I would like to be-- I'm speaking about Yiddish, the Yiddish word. That very seldom, I have an opportunity to speak to someone in Yiddish, except maybe to my wife sometimes, to my children, once in a while. They understand a little Yiddish. My wife understands well, Yiddish. Sometimes my mother-in-law and her husband. Sometimes older people. I meet friends, I'm speaking Yiddish. Such a beautiful heritage, such a beautiful heritage-- the Yiddish literature is. It-- it-- it-- the songs and the operas was written in Yiddish-- and the plays written. And everything is under dust, under dust somewhere.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was painful. It's very painful. Matter of fact, right now, I am involved in poetry. I'm writing poetry in all three languages. Some of them are already published. And a lot of them have to do with, directly, indirect-- or indirectly, with the Holocaust, with Israel. I'm proud of Israel, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my-- my kids, my wife, is urging me to publish this officially, but I'll see. Maybe I'm going to write a book about what I was talking about today. Kids telling me, Daddy, you have to start to write a book. I said, when? I don't have time. Well-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Tell us about the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an enlarged portrait of my parents. Szlomo Szczupak and Maria Szczupak, from home, [? Buxenbaum, ?] I think, a few weeks before their marriage-- 1934. Picture was made in 1934. It's a picture before they got married. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture of me and my wife, Leah [? Strazinska, ?] who right just became Leah Szczupak this-- this day of our marriage. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June the 18th, 1967. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] This is a portrait of my children made in 1987-- Chana Szczupak and Steve Szczupak-- or Szlomo Szczupak. This picture was made as a gift for us, for the parents, for our 20th anniversary. [PAUSES FOR 20 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Because I made a mistake. OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my children, Steve Szczupak and Chana Szczupak, made a few years ago. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture made in 1945, a few months after my mother found out that my dad is alive. It was made, the picture, to send to my father. Here, in the middle, is my mother. And the-- the taller boy, it's me and the-- the shorter one is my brother, Simon. The outfits we're wearing is-- are made from a Russian soldier's uniform. My mother sew it for us. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] This is an enlargement of my daddy-- my father's portrait. He sent us this picture when he found out about us. Here, he is a Sergeant in the Russian Army. [PAUSES FOR 17 SECONDS] This is a picture made during-- during a examination, final examination of the high school, Yiddish high school. I am here, seen as a teacher of Yiddish, and a student is answering in Yiddish. And, of course, on the board, we see writings about Yiddish literature, what he knows. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a part of the faculty tableau of the Yiddish school, matter of fact, the last year of existence of the Yiddish school in Legnica, Poland. It was right in the middle of the anti-Semitic commotions in Poland. I am here, shown as Professor Jacob Szczupak. I was teaching then Russian and Yiddish. At the same tableau, on the bottom left, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bottom left here, is my youngest brother, Joseph Szczupak, in the same class. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture of me. I was approximately, here, two and a half, three years old. Before the war, in park in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a portrait of three of us, three brothers. I am in the middle. To the left is my youngest brother, Joseph. And to the right is my middle brother, Simon, right before he went to the Polish military. It was made in 1960. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture made in 1947. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Poland, exists also a Jewish scouting organization, which I belonged to. This is a reunion of scouts from two different Yiddish schools, from Zlotoryja and Legnica. And here I am, that's me there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually, I'm sorry, Jim. We're going to use it. Yeah, I figured why not? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my wife when I saw her the first time. The girl to the left, the smiling one, that's my wife, my future wife, working in-- in a summer camp-- working, helping the farmers in their harvest. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the same time, in the same summer camp, here we see Jewish children in a bus going somewhere. And I'm standing in the middle, singing with the kids, apparently, Yiddish songs. Teaching them how to sing and having a good time. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture made a few weeks after we arrived in United States, in Atlanta. This is the first picture made in United States. That's my son, the little boy, me, and my wife-- covering herself, because she was pregnant with my daughter. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This is a picture of a part of my Russian class in the Cobb County School system, recently made. We are preparing for-- we are in a rehearsal for Russian songs and dancing, because in our class, we're also doing this. And here I am, in a Russian outfit. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, camera is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Szczupak, who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's my-- they're the most precious thing-- things. That's my wife, Lucy Szczupak. For 29 years, she went with me everywhere, in good times and bad times. She's a real hero in my-- my eyes. And this is my son, Steve-- or in Yiddish, Szlomo, after my father's name. He's 28 years old. We are proud of him. He's helping my wife. He's the manager of the-- the business my wife has. And unfortunately, my daughter could not be here. She had different plans. Her name is Chana Szczupak. You saw her on a picture. She's 26 years old. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody in the family wants to say anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I could say a lot. I-- I don't know exactly-- I haven't been here for the whole taping. But as far as the-- the survivors are concerned, I consider myself a survivor, as well. That may seem arrogant, but in my view, anyone-- I was born in Poland. And I was not supposed to be. Let's leave it at that. Right now, there are less Jews in Poland than-- than you can even count. There's just no one left. And to be born after the war in Poland, I think, is kind of odd. And not just being from Poland, but being a young Jewish person and still around, with this whole Jewish movement, I just consider every Jew a survivor if we are here still. And not just through this particular-- but throughout history, people have tried to wipe out the Jewish people. And they haven't been able to and there's something to be said for that. So, we're all survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually, what Steve was referring to as not supposed to be-- we were very, touched, too, and at his bar mitzvah, Rabbi Feldman-- Emmanuel Feldman-- was the one that named his bar mitzvah as especially precious and special, due to the fact that he is a grandson of an Auschwitz survivor. And that's where he took those words from-- the bar mitzvah that was not supposed to be, according to plans of Hitler.
